General safety and regulatory
Conventions for safety and regulatory information
1.1 Conventions for safety and regulatory information
1.1.1 Conventions for warnings, cautions, and notes
The conventions give information about the class and the level of the risk in the warnings and
cautions, and the content of notes.
WARNING 1.1
A warning is information about procedures, tasks, or conditions with a risk of fatal or serious
injury, injury, or clinical mistreatment, if you do not obey the instructions.
CAUTION 1.1
A caution is information about procedures, tasks, or conditions with a risk of damage to the
equipment, data, or the environment, if you do not obey the instructions.
Note: A note gives more information about a related text.

If necessary, contact Elekta Limited for more information on the applicable safety standards.

General safety and regulatory
Important safety instruction
1.4 Important safety instruction
It is important to install, operate, and do the maintenance correctly on all the equipment. This is
most important for safety related items. For the safety of the User and patients, Elekta
recommends that the User read, understand, and obey:
• All warnings, cautions, and notes in this document and related documents
• All warnings, cautions, and safety markings on the equipment and the accessories of
the equipment
• All safety instructions and information in this document and related documents.
Elekta recommends that:
• All Users obey the precautions in this section
• All Users examine the warnings, cautions, and notes in this document and use them in
their local Work Instructions.
Before the User operates, or do work on the equipment, it is important that they read and
understand the emergency procedures.
1.4.1 Using this document
WARNING 1.3
Do not use this document to operate the equipment or to do the repair and maintenance work.
If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury or clinical mistreatment.
Only use this document together with the applicable user and service documentation. Before you
operate the equipment or do the repair and maintenance work, read the warnings, cautions and
safety precautions in the Instructions for Use and corrective maintenance documentation.
1.4.2 Safety precautions for the compatibility of the equipment
WARNING 1.4
Do not use parts or accessories unless they are supplied or approved by Elekta. If you ignore
this warning, you can cause fatal injury, clinical mistreatment, and damage to the equipment.
Only operate the equipment with Elekta supplied or approved, compatible equipment or parts.
Accessories, transducers, and cables that are not specified by or do not have the approval of
Elekta can cause an incorrect treatment delivery. They can also have an effect on the
performance of the electromagnetic compatibility (EMC), which can increase the emissions or
decrease the immunity of the equipment.
Contact Elekta for information about the compatibility of other equipment or parts.
1.4.3 Safety precautions for the electromagnetic compatibility
The design and manufacture of Elekta products is in compliance with applicable EMC standards.
The intended result of the standards is to minimize the risk of electromagnetic interference by RF
devices. The type of device and the distance to the equipment has an effect on the intensity of
the electromagnetic interference. The equipment includes the linear accelerator and accessories,
Elekta Medical Linear Accelerator Document ID: 4513 370 2090 07
Corrective Maintenance Manual for System Communications
©2019 Elekta Limited. Page 11

General safety and regulatory
Important safety instruction
the control cabinets, the computers in the control room, and equipment from other
manufacturer.
It is necessary to have special precautions for EMC emissions. Elekta recommends that you obey
these precautions when you use RF devices near the equipment.
• Do not use RF devices unless they are in compliance with international standards for
electromagnetic compatibility, for example, the CE or FCC mark.
• Do not use a two-way emergency radio in the treatment room or the control room.
Only use an emergency radio if there is an emergency.
• Do not use cellular telephones, or other devices with cellular modems near the
equipment. The Instructions for Use contains information on the minimum safe
distance of these devices from the equipment.
• When you use a device that is enabled for wireless operation, it must be a minimum of
10 cm from the equipment.
• Radio-Frequency Identification (RFID) readers, when activated to do a scan of a RFID
tag, must be a minimum of one meter from the equipment.
Note: The RFID systems that Elekta supply use lower power levels. You can safely use these RFID
systems when you obey the procedures in the Instructions for Use for the product.
The system must be installed, put into service, and maintained in compliance with the EMC
information in the Instructions for Use.
Contact Elekta for information about the compatibility of parts and equipment.
WARNING 1.5
Do not put portable or mobile radio frequency (RF) communication devices near to the
equipment. The emissions from such RF devices can be more than the EMC standards, and can
have unwanted effects on the equipment operation. If you ignore this warning, the RF
emissions can cause the equipment to have a malfunction. A malfunction can cause fatal injury,
clinical mistreatment, or damage to the equipment.
WARNING 1.6
Do not use accessories, transducers, and cables that are not specified by Elekta. They can have
an effect on the EMC performance, which can increase EMC emissions or decrease the
immunity to RF interference of the equipment. If you ignore this warning, incorrect radiation
delivery and other malfunctions can occur, which can cause fatal injury or clinical
mistreatment.
1.4.4 Safety precautions for operation of the equipment
WARNING 1.7
Do not operate the equipment unless the QA checks are satisfactorily completed. If you ignore
this warning, you can cause fatal injury, clinical mistreatment, and damage to the equipment.
WARNING 1.8
Do not operate the equipment if it is damaged, defective, or incorrectly calibrated. If you
ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury, clinical mistreatment, and damage to the
equipment.
Document ID: 4513 370 2090 07 Elekta Medical Linear Accelerator

General safety and regulatory
Important safety instruction
Only operate the equipment if you are a qualified person with the applicable authority. Such
operation must apply best practice for clinical treatment and obey the laws and regulations in the
jurisdiction in which the equipment is installed.
Before you operate the equipment, make sure that:
• There are no defective parts.
• The equipment calibration is correct.
• The applicable QA checks are satisfactorily completed.
• The user routine checks are satisfactorily completed.
• The planned maintenance schedule is completed satisfactorily.
For information on the user routine checks and the planned maintenance schedule, see the
applicable section of the Instructions for Use.
It is the responsibility of the User to set up a QA program that gives safe and satisfactory system
operation.
1.4.5 Safety precaution for Service Tools and lifting equipment
WARNING 1.9
Do not use incorrectly calibrated or defective Service Tools. If you ignore this warning, you can
cause injury and damage to the equipment.
WARNING 1.10
Do not use defective or damaged lifting equipment. If you ignore this warning, you can cause
fatal injury and damage to the equipment.
Before you use the Service Tool or lifting equipment, make sure that:
• There are no defective or damaged parts
• The calibration of the Service Tool is correct
• The applicable calibration certificate is valid
• The lifting equipment gives the maximum Safe Working Load (SWL).
For information on the maintenance and calibration schedules of the Service Tool, see the
Instructions for Use and Calibration Certificate for the tool.
1.4.6 Safety precaution for work with safety devices during service
operation
WARNING 1.11
Do not remove, change, or override the safety switches, interlocks, or other safety devices
unless special instructions in this document tell you to. If you ignore this warning, you can
cause fatal injury.
The procedures in this document can tell you to remove, change, or override the safety switches,
interlocks, or other safety devices during repair or maintenance work. When the work is
completed, make all the safety devices active again, and do a test of them.

General safety and regulatory
Important safety instruction
1.4.7 Safety precautions for work with electrical and mechanical parts
WARNING 1.12
Do not start to do work on the linear accelerator before you isolate the power to the work area
at the interface cabinet. You can get a high voltage electric shock. If you ignore this warning, it
can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 1.13
Do not remove the covers or cables from the equipment unless special instructions in this
document tell you to. The covers give access to high voltage electric parts. Always install the
covers and cables again before you use the equipment. If you ignore this warning, it can cause
fatal injury.
WARNING 1.14
Do not remove the covers from the equipment unless special instructions in this document tell
you to. The covers give access to parts that move. Always install the covers again before you
use the equipment. If you ignore this warning, it can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 1.15
Be careful when the power supply switch of the equipment is put in the off position. There
continues to be a risk of high voltage electric shock. Some of the customer interfaces can
continue to supply electrical power to the electrical terminals. If you ignore this warning, it can
cause fatal injury.
Only operate the equipment in rooms that are in compliance with the applicable electrical safety
laws and regulations for this type of equipment.
Before you start the maintenance and repair work, or start to clean the equipment, isolate the
electrical power supply and prevent unwanted movement of the equipment.
1.4.8 Safety precautions for work with UV devices
WARNING 1.16
Do not look into the ultraviolet lighting of the UV device without protective glasses. If you
ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.
Some devices use ultraviolet lighting. The covers and screens of the device contain the ultraviolet
lighting to the inner parts of the device.
If there is damage to the covers or screen of the UV device, switch off the digital accelerator and
contact your Elekta representative.

General safety and regulatory
Important safety instruction
1.4.9 Safety precautions for work with laser devices
WARNING 1.17
Do not look into the laser beams. The result can be damage to your eyes. If you ignore this
warning, it can cause serious injury.
1.4.10 Safety precaution for work with display screen equipment
WARNING 1.18
Do not use the display screen equipment for long periods of time. If you ignore this warning,
you can cause injury.
When you use the display screen equipment (DSE), obey the applicable Health and Safety
regulations and procedures in the jurisdiction where you do the work.
1.4.11 Safety precaution for the installation of accessories on the BLD
WARNING 1.19
Do not touch the connector found in the accessory ring on the BLD. If you ignore this warning,
you can cause injury.
When you install an accessory, for example, a shadow tray, electron applicator, or front pointer,
do not touch the pins in the connector. The pins have a low electrical current, which can give an
electric shock when you touch them.
1.4.12 Precautions for work with electrostatic discharge sensitive devices
CAUTION 1.2
Do not touch the PCBs and other electronic parts unless you wear a grounded antistatic wrist
strap. If you ignore this caution, you can cause damage to the equipment.
CAUTION 1.3
Do not touch the parts identified with the ESD caution label. Always obey the ESD precautions
during work on these parts. If you ignore this caution, you can cause damage to the equipment.
Semiconductor devices, integrated circuits, and the parts that contain them, are ESD sensitive
devices. These devices are electrostatic discharge sensitive (ESDS) devices. ESD can cause damage
to these devices and cause them to have a malfunction immediately or some time after the
electrostatic discharge. To prevent the ESD damage to these devices, always wear a grounded
antistatic wrist strap, and obey the applicable ESD precautions and procedures.
Elekta recommends that all users receive the training about ESD. The training must include the
ESD caution label and these ESD precautions.

General safety and regulatory
Important safety instruction
1.4.13 Safety precautions for work with heavy parts
WARNING 1.20
Do not lift or move heavy parts unless you obey the applicable Health and Safety regulations
and procedures. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.
1.4.14 Safety precautions for cleaning and disinfection
WARNING 1.21
Do not clean the equipment, or use a disinfectant spray when the equipment is connected to
the electrical supply. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 1.22
Do not use a spray that is explosive or flammable. If you ignore this warning, you can cause
fatal injury and damage to the equipment.
CAUTION 1.4
Do not let water and other liquids go into the equipment. If you ignore this caution, you can
cause damage to the equipment.
CAUTION 1.5
Do not use a spray to clean the medical equipment room. If you ignore this caution, you can
cause damage to the equipment.
At regular intervals, it is necessary to clean the equipment and equipment room with
disinfectants. Elekta recommends that the procedures to clean the equipment and the
equipment room obey the applicable laws and regulations of the jurisdiction where the
equipment is installed.
1.4.15 Safety precaution for the occurrence of fire or explosion
WARNING 1.23
Do not use the equipment in rooms that contain explosive or flammable gases. If you ignore
this warning, you can cause fatal injury and damage to the equipment.
High levels of oxygen, and some anesthetic gases and disinfectant sprays, can make an explosive
or flammable environment in the room.
1.4.16 General cleaning of the equipment
At regular intervals, it is necessary to clean the equipment and equipment room. This section
gives information on the general procedures. Where special procedures and precautions are
necessary, the related documents give the information on these procedures.
Clean surfaces of the equipment with a mild detergent solution. Then, dry the surfaces with a
clean, lint-free cloth.
Document ID: 4513 370 2090 07 Elekta Medical Linear Accelerator
Corrective Maintenance Manual for System Communications
Page 16 ©2019 Elekta Limited.

General safety and regulatory
Important safety instruction
Do not let the sprays, water, or other liquids go into the equipment. These can cause electrical
short circuits, metal corrosion, and other damage.
Do not use a solution, solvent, abrasive detergent, or polish that is corrosive. If you do not know
the properties of the solution, do not use it.
1.4.17 Recommendations for emergency procedures
Before you use the equipment, Elekta recommends that you know all the local emergency
procedures and the locations of all the emergency switches.
The Elekta equipment includes:
• The STOP MOTORS buttons on the handheld controllers and user interface panels in
the treatment room, and FKP in the control room.
When you press these buttons, it disconnects the electrical supply from the motors,
stops all movements of the equipment, and stops kV and MV radiation.
• The EMERGENCY POWER OFF buttons in the treatment room, control area, and areas
near the equipment.
When you operate these buttons, it disconnects the electrical supply to the linear
accelerator. The buttons disconnect the electrical supply from many electrical circuits,
but not from all the circuits.
1.4.18 Disposal of the equipment
WARNING 1.24
Do not discard radioactive and hazardous material through the domestic waste systems or in
the environment. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury and damage to the
environment.
End of life (EOL) disposal is the procedure to disassemble, remove, and discard the equipment, in
full or in parts, to a condition where it is not possible to operate the equipment for its intended
use.
Always use procedures that give the best possible protection to the environment when you
remove and discard the equipment. The procedures must obey the laws and regulations of the
jurisdictions in which you discard the equipment. Do not discard Elekta equipment through the
domestic waste systems.
Only a Qualified Expert (QE) and an approved facility with an applicable license, must remove and
discard the equipment and recycle the material. Do not discard material that is hazardous to
health and the environment together with other material.
Before you remove and discard equipment, contact Elekta for information.

Introduction
CMM Document scope
2.1 CMM Document scope
The corrective maintenance manual (CMM) helps service engineers troubleshoot, repair and
maintain the systems, subsystems and components of the linear accelerator. The target audience
is qualified service engineers. These may be engineers employed directly by Elekta, qualified
representatives of Elekta or qualified representatives of Elekta's customers.
The manual provides:

Product Description of the System Communications
Overview of the system communications system
3.1 Overview of the system communications system
The system communications system transmits the TCS command signals to printed circuit boards
(PCBs) in three different control areas:
• Radiation head control area (RHCA) - AREA 12
• HT and RF control area (HTCA) - AREA 16
• Interface cabinet control area (ICCA) - AREA 72.
The PCBs send the TCS command signals to the related user systems. The PCBs also send
feedback data from the user systems to the TCS. This two-way communication lets the TCS
accurately control the operation of the linear accelerator during treatment.
3.2 Description of the system communications
The real time software uses the LynxOS operating system in the control processor of the
treatment control system (TCS) to control the linear accelerator.
The TCS communicates with the linear accelerator through the two serial data links A and B,
which operate independently. The TCS communicates with the beam limiting device (BLD)
through a third serial link and a dedicated video cable. See the corrective maintenance manual
for the BLD for more information.
The serial link terminates at the serial link isolation PCBs in the treatment control cabinet (TCC) in
AREA 92. At the linear accelerator, the serial data links connect to the control areas which
operate independently. The control areas are:
• Radiation head control area (RHCA) - AREA 12
• HT and RF control area (HTCA) - AREA 16
• Interface cabinet control area (ICCA) - AREA 72.
The control areas supply the command signals and data from the TCS to the other systems of the
linear accelerator. They also send read/write type command data and other information to the
TCS.
Each of the control areas has a PCB rack with a backplane and a set of PCBs. The control areas
divide into two remote terminal units (RTU) A and B. Each RTU has an internal power supplies.
Therefore, each RTU operate independently the interlocks and safety circuits that they control.
In the control areas, the PCBs in each RTU communicate with each other across a dedicated bus.
The A side bus and the B side bus are isolated from each other. The RTU bus does not connect
between the control areas. The backplane gives the interconnection between the control areas
and the other systems of the linear accelerator.
The system communications has three different types of connections:
• The serial data links A and B between the TCS and the control areas
• The bus between the PCBs in an RTU
• The backplane interconnections between the control areas and the linear accelerator.
See the Figure 3.1 for information about the system communications.

The control areas contain the RTUs. Each of the control areas has two RTUs. Each RTU always

Each of the control area racks always includes three PCBs:
• Analog output 8-bit (AO8) PCBs

• Auxiliary PSU PCB.
The function of the control area gives the other PCBs that are installed. For example, the HTCA
contains the programmable pulse generator (PPG) PCB that is necessary for the HT and RF
systems.
The ribbon cables externally connect the SCC and AI12 PCBs in each RTU. The RTU bus

3.4.2 Description of the programmable logic devices
The DIE PCB has two programmable logic devices that control the linear accelerator interlocks
and movements. Each of the PLDs has different firmware to do the applicable functions of the
DIE PCB in that RTU. Because of this, each of the DIE PCBs has a different part number and cannot
be interchanged with other DIE PCBs (during the troubleshooting, for example).
A number of the output signals from the PLDs control the relays in the interlock chains of the
linear accelerator. If a dangerous condition occurs, one or more of the PLDs operates
immediately to break the applicable interlock chain. This disables the linear accelerator.
Other output signals from the PLDs give the control of machine movements. When a movement
operates a limit switch, the related PLD operates immediately to switch off the motor drive and
prevent more movement.
The PLDs are very important to the operation of the control system. The devices check 100
machine state signals and send a signal in 50 µs. Therefore, each of the PLDs can switch on or
switch off a total of 48 machine control signals.
3.4.3 Description of the PCB rack
A PCB rack for each of the control areas contains the PCBs.
The rack has the card slots for a maximum of 18 PCBs and a panel for plugs and sockets. Each of
the racks contains 16 Eurocard (Double) PCBs. Two of the card slot locations, (K and M), you
cannot use.
The PCBs are configured in two groups:
• The PCBs in card slots A to J are connected to the data bus for RTU A (left side)
• The PCBs in card slots L to V are connected to the data bus for RTU B (right side).
Each of the card slots has a connector number in the range from 1 to 83.
3.4.4 Description of the PCB identification code
The PCB identification code clearly identifies a PCB for installation in a specified control area of
the linear accelerator. The PCB identification code can use acronyms or acronyms and numbers
to identify the PCB.
An acronym PCB identification code identifies the PCB type and control area in which the PCB is
installed. For example:
• DIE for the digital input and encoding PCB
• RTU A for remote terminal unit A
• HTA for high tension control area A.
Therefore, the identification code for the multiplexer terminal unit in RTU A of HT control area A
is MTU-HTA.
The PCB identification code for motor drive areas uses acronyms and numerals. For example:

3.5 Description of the standard printed circuit boards
The linear accelerator contains many printed circuit boards (PCBs). Some PCBs are only used in

There are also PCBs that all the systems of the linear accelerator use. The PCBs communicate the

3.7.2 Operation of the MTU PCB circuits
The multiplex terminal unit (MTU) PCBs control the serial communications interface between the
treatment control cabinet (TCC) and the remote terminal units (RTU) in the three control areas of
the linear accelerator. The MTU PCB controls the communications at the linear accelerator end of
the serial link.
The treatment control system (TCS) transmits the addresses and data through the serial link to
the linear accelerator. All the MTU PCBs read this information. The MTU PCB in the correct RTU
decodes the received data and addresses and puts the information on the RTU bus. The MTU
then sends an echo and status report to the TCS.
The MTU includes an FPGA (IC3) that gets its program from the start EPROM (IC1) at power up.
The FPGA does these functions of the MTU:
• An ISO standard CAN interface as a replacement for the military standard 1553 serial
link.
• A IEEE JTAG test interface
• An Altera Byte Blaster™ interface.
Each of the MTUs changes the serial/parallel data and encodes the address for the PCBs
connected to one data bus in a control area. Therefore:
• MTU-RHA, MTU-HTA and MTU-ICA do the communications for the RTU A data buses
• MTU-RHB, MTU-HTB and MTU-ICB do the communications for the RTU B data buses.
The MTU PCB monitors the serial link for a valid command synchronization signal and one valid
data bit (Manchester II encoding). When it receives these, the MTU PCB does a check for bit or
parity errors in the command word. If it is valid, the MTU PCB does a check of the transmitted
RTU address. If the address is correct, the PCB does a check to find out if a read or a write
command is sent.

3.7.2.1 Operation of the MTU PCB read command
When the MTU PCB senses a read command, it supplies the necessary signals on the parallel bus
and receives the data from the applicable PCB in the rack. The PCB then sends back the data
encoded with parity.
3.7.2.2 Operation of the MTU PCB write command
When the MTU PCB senses a write command, the MTU PCB pauses to get a valid data
synchronization and one valid data bit. If valid, the MTU PCB supplies the necessary signals to
load the data into the applicable PCB in that RTU.

3.7.2.3 Operation of the MTU PCB read/write complete
When the system completes a read or write cycle, the MTU PCB sends a status word to the TCS to
show that the transfer was successful. If the transfer was not successful, the LEDs on the MTU
PCB give information for the troubleshooting.
3.7.2.4 Operation of the MTU PCB watchdog timer
A watchdog timer gives a timeout if the system has no active data movement for 1.6 seconds,
and a 140 ms (minimum) reset at system start.
3.7.2.5 Operation of the MTU PCB - serial interfaces
The field programmable gate array (FPGA) IC 3 controls the 1553 bus, the CAN bus and the local
linear accelerator controller bus.
The 1553 bus serial interface is through transformer T1.
IC6 gives the CAN interface. The serial input and output goes through two optical isolators (IC8
and IC9) and the bus output driver (IC17). Two output connectors give the CAN connections.
3.7.2.6 Description of the RTU addresses
Each of the remote terminal units (RTU) on the serial link connects to a dedicated address
between 0 and 5. The RTU addresses are set in the hardware of the backplane of each RTU.
Therefore, the multiplex terminal unit (MTU) PCB automatically uses the correct address when it
is installed in the card slot.
The address is set with three pins on the MTU connector (PL2, c27 to PL2, c29). These pins are set
to 0 V as is necessary (see list: Addresses on the MTU RTU). The MTU PCB on the adjacent
connector row (pins PL2, a27 to PL2, a29) gives the 0 V.

3.8.2 Operation of the digital input and encoding PCB
The DIE PCB decodes a number of inputs to control a smaller number of outputs. Each of the DIE
PCBs is in a pair with an adjacent ROC PCB.
The DIE PCB has two primary functions:
• To change the digital signals from the linear accelerator to the TCS
• To monitor the digital inputs to give a local function in each remote terminal unit (RTU)
that operates independently from the TCS.
The DIE PCBs are electrically the same. Each DIE PCB has two field programmable logic devices
(PLD) that control the specified functions of that PCB. Therefore each DIE PCB has a specified
function and location and cannot be interchanged with other DIE PCBs (for example, for the
troubleshooting).
See Figure 3.5 for a block diagram of the DIE PCB.

3.8.2.1 Operation of the DIE PCB input circuits
A limit is set on each of the digital input signals. The signal is then filtered and applied to a
comparator. The comparator gives the level (threshold), at which the input signal causes a TTL
level output from the DIE PCB.
Fixed reference inputs
The 16 inputs (PL2 a2 to a9 and PL2 c2 to c9) are applied to an array of four comparators (IC D2
to IC D5), with four inputs to each comparator. The level of all four comparators is fixed at +10 V.
When the level of the input is equal to or is more than +10 V, the related output of the
comparator is +5 V (high). When the input level is less than +10 V, the related output is 0 V (low).
Selected reference inputs
Two of the digital inputs (PL2 a13 and PL2 a14) are applied to a comparator (IC D1 pin 5 and IC D1
pin 7). The level of operation for each input is set by an external signal (PL2 a20). If the level of an
input signal is more than that of the external signal, the output of the related comparator is high.
Programmed reference level
Two of the digital input signals (PL2 a12 and PL2 c12) are applied to different comparators, the
levels of which are set by software. The level value is sent as a data byte on lines D0 to D7. The
data is changed to an analog value by two DACs. This value sets the same level on the two
comparators. This circuit is used for the vacuum release level setting in the HTCA (AREA 16).
The removal of the links CL7 1 and CL7 2, gives hardware configuration of the release levels with
pull up/down resistors.
3.8.2.2 Operation of the DIE PCB programmable logic devices
Two PLDs give the local functions of the DIE PCBs. Each PLD each has 16 inputs and eight outputs.
The program is special to each DIE PCB. Therefore each device has two 4-bit outputs: one to
control external relays and the other to feedback to the TCS.
3.8.2.3 Operation of the DIE PCB control system communications
The TCS directly controls some channels of the DIE PCB, other channels use local inputs to send
data to the TCS.
• State control The TCS has a system of items to control the machine state of the linear
accelerator. The state items read from, and write to, the PLDs on the DIE PCBs.
– View state items these items (i245 to i249) send the condition of the RTUs to the
control system. The state items get this information from the PLDs.
– Global state the global state items (i494 to i499) send a 4-bit code to the PLDs to
control the state of the linear accelerator. The global states make sure that the
linear accelerator is at a constant level across all RTUs.
3.8.2.4 Operation of the DIE PCB reset
The STROBE signal continuously operates the watchdog timer. If the STROBE signal does not
occur in 150 mS, the timer makes a RESET signal. This signal clears the latch that holds the byte
and the latches of the logic array processor for the programmable inputs block.

3.9.3 Operation of the ROC PCB circuits
The relay output card (ROC) PCB has switches for 17 output channels from a number of different
inputs:
• The three TTL inputs, which controls the three relays RLA1, RLA2, and RLA3. The
relays make the change between 0 V and +26 V for bipolar motor control. The
relays also operate the contactors and solenoids.
• The data bits D1 to D7 from the control area data bus that operate seven output
channels:
– Bits D1, D2, and D3 control three Darlington outputs, each of which gives 0 V at
500 mA to operate the relays and buzzers.
– Bits D4, D5, and D6 control three PNP transistor drivers, each of which gives +22 V
at 600 mA to operate the relays.
– Bit D7 controls relay RLB, which makes it possible to change the contacts to pins
on PL2.
• The four TTL inputs, which controls the transistor drivers of the four PNPs that
control the +22 V at 600 mA to operate the relays.
• The two TTL inputs, which control relays RLB3 and RLD1 that are used in the
interlock circuits.
• One relay (RLB2) gets its power directly from contacts on connector PL2 and gives
the normally-open contacts to connectors on PL2.
The ROC PCB also monitors four input conditions and writes the status on the data bus:
• It writes the two AC input status to data bits D4 and DS
• It writes the status of the board clamp interlock to bit D6
• It writes the +22 V supply status to bit D7.
Each ROC PCB has an adjacent DIE PCB. The TTL inputs are from the FPGAs on the related DIE
PCB, or from the DOS PCBs in the dosimetry system (see Linear Accelerator Corrective
Maintenance Manual - Dosimetry System).

• The normally-open contacts connect power to the motor.
The ROC-HTA, ROC-RHA and ROC-RHB PCBs use two of the relays to give positive movement,
negative movement or activate the brakes. This is controlled by the connection of the relay
contacts to the positive and negative terminals of the motor.
The ROC-HTB only uses relay RLB2 to give the vacuum 10-4 interlock in the gun filament control
circuit. An external control can energize RLB2. If it is, the PNP-6 driver on the same PCB operates
RLB2. This is controlled by the PLDs on DIE-HTB through pin PL2 a25.
The HT2 and movements interlock circuits use relay RLB3. In ROC-RHA and ROC-RHB, the HT
enable signal from pin PL2 a16 of the DOS-A and DOS-B PCBs energizes the relay RLB3. In ROC-
HTA, ROC-HTB and ROC-ICB, the PLDs on the adjacent DIE PCBs energize RLB3.
• Relay RLD1 on each ROC PCB are connected in series in a PRF interlock circuit. Two of
these circuits are installed:
– The first goes through RLD1 on all the ROC-A PCBs
– The second goes through RLD1 on all the ROC-Bs plus RLA1 on the processor

The PLDs on the adjacent DIE PCB energize relay RLD1 through pin PL2 A28.

Transistor TR7 is not used.
Transistor TR8 is used in all RTUs (A and B) to operate the interlock relays in the HT1 (CON- J)

The touch guard circuit uses transistor Tr10. The PLDs (through ICE1) on the adjacent DIE PCB
energize the transistor.
Processor outputs Write bit 7 energizes relay RLB1 through buffer ICF1. This gives a one terminal

3.9.3.4 Operation of the ROC PCB watchdog timer
Watchdog timer
To make sure that the ROC-PCB operates correctly, the STROBE signal continuously operates a
watchdog timer (ICM3). If the STROBE signal does not occur in 150 ms, the timer makes a reset
signal through ICM1.
Power up reset At power up TR1 clears the inputs data buffer ICK1, resets the ROC PCB and
momentarily keeps a low RESET signal. The ROC PCB is therefore set to a safe condition, with the
relays de-energized.

3.10.2 Operation of the analog input 12-bit PCB circuits
The AI12 PCB changes one of 62 analog input channels to a digital word that it sends to the TCS.
Each AI12 PCB operates with a SCC PCB, which adapts the analog signals into a specified range of
smooth inputs for the AI12 PCB.
The AI12 PCB measures the input voltage of a selected input channel from the adjacent SCC PCB
and changes this value into a digital output. A channel address from the TCS controls the
multiplexers and the ADC, which do the change. A 10 V voltage regulator and buffer supply the
reference voltage for the potentiometers that uses the AI12 PCB.
The voltage of the inputs from the SCC PCB is in the range 0 V to 10 V full scale range (FSR), or 0 V
to 1 V FSR.
The output is a 12-bit digital word in the range 0 to 4095 (decimal) (see Figure 3.7).

data words are received from the TCS on data bus D0 to D11 and put in the RAM. An address
generator uses an up down counter to add an address to the RAM and activate the related
sample and hold circuit. The DAC read, and if it is necessary, change the content of each address
and keep it in the sample and hold circuits.

3.12.2.1 Operation of the AO12 PCB data buffer
The 12-bit data word D0 to D11 inputs from the TCS are inputs to buffers before the distribution
to other circuits on the AO12 PCB.
3.12.2.2 Operation of the AO12 PCB RAM
The RAM array holds eight 12-bit data words. The memory locations are read in cycles in
synchronization with the sample and hold circuits.
The write delay circuit controls the RAM with dedicated write and enable signals. A PCB enable
signal from the control latch prevents the outputs of the RAM from random levels when power is
connected or during a system reset.
The RAM address circuit (ICN2) supplies a 3-bit read address.
3.12.2.3 Operation of the AO12 PCB RAM address
The RAM has two address modes:
• Refresh mode: In this mode, the RAM cycle through eight location addresses that a 3-
bit counter (ICN2) selects. The counter also controls a de-multiplexer (ICM3) that
activates the applicable sample and hold circuit.
• Write mode: In this mode, counter ICN2 is loaded with the address lines and held in this
mode during the write cycle of the bus. This makes it possible for address bits C0 to C2
from the TCS to go to the RAM address directly.
3.12.2.4 Operation of the AO12 PCB digital to analog converter
The RAM sends a 12-bit data word to the DAC. The DAC changes it to an analog voltage in the
range 0 V to 10 V and sends the analog output to the sample and hold circuit.
3.12.2.5 Operation of the AO12 PCB sample and hold circuit
The sample and hold circuit gives the storage for the values of the analog input voltage from the
DAC. The circuit makes and buffer eight dedicated outputs (IO0 to IO7) to the TCS. The outputs
are current values in the range 0 mA to 10 mA.
With different buffers, the IO0 to IO2 outputs also give signals VO0 to VO2, which are voltage
values in the range -5 V to +5 V.
3.12.2.6 Operation of the AO12 PCB address decoder
The board address decoder receives the address lines C0 to C6 and signals R/W and STROBE
when a message comes from the TCS. The circuit decodes these signals to give the outputs that:

3.12.2.7 Operation of the AO12 PCB write delay circuit
The write delay circuit makes sure that the RAM address is valid before a write cycle is activated.
The board address decoder sends an enable signal to the write delay circuit, which then sends a

3.12.2.8 Operation of the AO12 PCB control latch circuit
The control latch receives data bits D0 to D7 from the input buffer. The latch uses bits D0 to D2 to
control the monitor multiplexer. Bit D7 provides the BOARD ENABLE signal to the RAM.
3.12.2.9 Operation of the AO12 PCB multiplexer circuit
The monitor multiplexer selects one of the eight current channels (IO0 to IO7) to be the output
through (PL1, pin C28). The system uses this output to do troubleshooting through the AI12 PCB
in the applicable control area. The bits D0 to D2 from the control latch select the output.
3.12.2.10 Operation of the AO12 PCB watchdog timer
The STROBE signal starts the watchdog timer. The circuit times out if it is not started again in 150
ms. This causes a RESET signal and sets the outputs to a safe level, 0 mA or 0 V.

Product Description of the System Communications
Description of the universal motor drive (UMD) PCBs
Figure 3.11 Circuits on the universal motor drive PCBs
In the voltage regulator circuit, the internal ±15 V power supplies for the UMD PCB come from
the ±26 V power supplies, through standard 1 A regulators (IC4 and IC5). Fusible resistors (R1 and
R2) prevent damage to the PCB if a fault occurs, and decrease dissipation in the regulators.
In the current sensor circuit, the shunt resistors (R50 to R53) sense the output current. Resistor
R53 is always in the circuit with the load. Resistors R50 to R52 can be in or out of the cicuit. The
maximum necessary output current controls the links (in or out of the circuit) for resistors R50 to
R52 (the links are part of the rack wiring, and are therefore external to the UMD PCB). A
differential amplifier (IC1B) with high common mode rejection ratio (CMRR), and IC1C, increases
the shunt resistor voltage by a factor of 6.8. This gives a voltage at PL1, pin a14 in proportion to
the sensed current.
In the current limit circuit, Zener diodes (D5 and D6) control the output from the current sensor
circuit when the output becomes ±7.5 V (6.8 V + 0.7 V). This decreases the demand signal to IC1D
Elekta Medical Linear Accelerator Document ID: 4513 370 2090 07
Corrective Maintenance Manual for System Communications
©2019 Elekta Limited. Page 65

Product Description of the System Communications
System Description of the System Communications
and applies a limit to the output current. When this occurs, the sense voltage across resistors R50
to R53 is 1.1 V.
In the IR compensation circuit, the current sensor output for the motor drives goes to one of the
IR compensation inputs (IR COMP 1 to IR COMP 5), which subtracts the current sensor output
from a sample of the output voltage in amplifier IC1A. The output of the amplifier is a voltage
that is in proportion to the speed of the motor. The circuit also uses this voltage to adjust for wire
and armature resistance.
In the error amplifier circuit, the DEMAND input (PL1, pin a9), with a range of ±5.0 V, adds to the
FEEDBACK at pin c9 or c10 to supply the gain necessary. The error amplifier (IC1D) increases the
result of the added signals and sends an output to a pulse-width modulator.
In the pulse-width modulator circuit, a 110 kHz triangular wave generator (IC2D and IC3B) sends
its output of approximately ±6.0 V (peak) to comparators IC2A and IC2B. Diodes D7 and D8 apply
a 3 V level shift, which gives a 1 µs deadband to the back and front edges of the bipolar
modulator output. Diodes D3 and D4 limit the output voltage of IC1D to give approximately 11 V.
The R21 and C16 phase-shift network make the circuit more stable.
In the output stages circuit, the positive output from the modulator switches on TR4. This
switches on the parallel p-channel MOSFETs (TR1 and TR2) through diode D10. To switch on TR1
and TR2 fully, the potential divider (R35 and R36, and R37 to R39) makes the gate (in the
MOSFET) go 10 V below the source. An emitter follower (TR3) discharges the gate-source
capacitance of the MOSFETs to switch them off. The typical time to switch on or off is 100 ns. A
configuration of the p-channel (D11 and R42 to R46) switches on and switches off the n-channel
MOSFETs (TR7 and TR8). Flyback diodes D12 and D13 limit the output to L1 during the deadband
period. An LC filter circuit (L1 and C20 to C22) with a threshold frequency of approximately 10
kHz controls the output to decrease unwanted, remaining, small changes in the output signal.
In the enable circuit, the ENABLE input signal at PL1 (pin c12) is active low. The input signal goes
to a comparator (IC2C) through a potential divider circuit (R31 to R33). In the disabled state, the
comparator gives a high output (pulled up by R34). The JFETs (TR9 and TR11) switch on to stop
the signal to the error amplifier and the output stage. When the ENABLE input is less than +5 V,
the output of the comparator (IC2C) changes to -15 V. This switches off the JFETs (TR9 and TR11)
to let the circuit operate.
In the power failure protection circuit, a comparator (IC2C) gives a high output that switches on
the JFETs (TR9 and TR11) to disable the UMD PCB if the -15 V supply fails. If the +15 V supply fails,
the gate to JFET TR10 goes to -15 V and TR10 switches on (fully). This stops the signal to the
output stage.
In the inverter circuit, when two UMD PCBs operate in a bridge, the input signals go to a unity
gain inverter (IC3C). The demand signal INVERTER INPUT goes to PL1 (pin a13) and the output
signal INVERTER OUTPUT comes from PL1 (pin c13). During bridge mode operation, the INVERTER
OUTPUT from one UMD PCB goes to the DEMAND input of the other UMD PCB of the pair.

3.15.2 Functions of the system communications system
3.15.2.1 Description of the data formats
The signals that go between each of the RTUs are a parallel format. The signals between the RTUs
and the TCS are through the serial link. Signals in the TCS are a parallel format. The TCS and the
RTUs of the control areas therefore include the circuits that:
• Change the serial signal inputs to parallel format
• Change parallel output signals to serial format.
The linac interface PCB (LIB) changes the format in the TCS.
The MTU PCBs changes the format in each of the control areas.
3.15.2.2 Description of the analog input signals
The TCS receives analog and digital feedback from the different subsystems of the linear
accelerator. The analog signals give the control and feedback for subsystems. These signals are
digitized in the RTUs before they are sent to the TCS. The AI12 PCBs in the control areas change
the analog signals to a digital format.

3.15.2.3 Description of the digital signal monitoring
The TCS uses digital signals to communicate with a number of PCBs in the control areas. The
communication is usually with systems that are monitored by switches, relays and contactors.
Therefore, it can be necessary to change the signal level of digital signals before the transmission
to the TCS. The digital input and encoding (DIE) PCBs and the relay output card (ROC) PCBs,
monitor the digital signals.
3.15.3 Description of the serial interfaces
The two serial links operate independently. They are the only command and control interface
between the TCC and the control areas of the linear accelerator. Serial link A operates with the
RTU on the A-side of each control area, and serial link B operates with the RTU on the B-side.
The TCS uses a primary/secondary protocol to communicate with the control areas. In this
communication, the primary protocol is the TCS interface, and the secondary protocol is the
control interface in the control areas .
3.15.3.1 Description of the MIL-STD-1553 standard
The binary serial link uses some of the MIL-STD-1553 Linac Assembly (USAF) standard, the
properties of which are:
• Signals are transferred along twisted-pair cables
• The signals are serial digital pulse code modulated
• The data transfer is asynchronous
• The communication is half duplex - an interface cannot transmit and receive at the
same time
• All control is of the command and feedback type, that software in the TCS starts
• Manchester II encoding is used.
The serial link is different to the MIL-STD-1553 standard, as follows:
• The command feedback time is in 200 µs
• The system can transmit only one data word in each access
• RTU to RTU transfers in the control areas are not used (transfers use the RTU bus).
3.15.3.1.1 Description of the serial interface connections
In the treatment control cabinet (TCC), serial interfaces A and B start at the LIB. These then
connect, through a ribbon cable, to the serial link isolation PCB. The serial interfaces are then
routed to the RTUs of the linear accelerator through sockets SK1 (serial link A) and SK2 (serial link
B) on the serial link isolation PCB.
The serial link cables between the RTUs connect in a chain in which the input label is Y and the
output label is X. At the RTUs, the serial link cables terminate at 15-way D-type plugs on the
connector panel of the related control area. Each plug is identified applicable to its cable: for
example, PL72YA is the connector of cable 72YA. The input cable of serial link A to RTU-A of the
ICCA.
See Table 3.26 and Table 3.27 for information about the serial link cables.

20 (parity bit) The MTU uses the parity bit to make sure that the command
word is valid. The total number of bits that are logic 1, which
includes the parity bit, is always an odd number.
3.15.3.2.2 Description of the analog output signals
The TCS sends analog demand signals to the control areas. Some outputs operate at a higher
resolution than other outputs. Therefore, the control areas contain two types of analog output
PCB:
• AO8 PCB - give the outputs at moderate (8-bit) resolution that are usually used for
the motor control systems.
• AO12 - give the outputs at high (12-bit) resolution that are used for parameters,
for example:
– The beam steering and the focus coils
– The beam bending coils
– The gun filament current
– The magnetron power conditions.
3.15.3.2.3 Description of the status word format
The TCS sends the 20-bit status word to the applicable RTU address. The status word is in the
format for serial interface data words (see Figure 3.16).

3.15.3.2.5 Description of the data validation
When the address for an MTU PCB is active, the MTUs do a check of the data on the serial
interface and make sure that it is valid. The check makes sure that:
• The word starts with a valid synchronization pulse
• The word bits are in the correct Manchester II code
• The parity is odd.
If a word is not valid, the MTU PCB resets until it receives a synchronization pulse that is valid.
3.15.3.2.6 Description of the output voltage
The output voltage of the serial link signals is ±3.0 V (±10%) peak from line, as measured at the
output of the MTU PCB.
3.15.3.3 Description of the read/write protocol
Each RTU in the control areas gets a command from the TCS. The TCS do checks to make sure
that the command on the serial link is valid and points to the applicable RTU. When the TCS
senses a command that is valid, the communication part of the RTU address becomes active.
When the MTU senses a command, it decodes the address in the command word. The MTU then
gets the data from the applicable channel of the PCB address. When the MTU has the data, it
sends a status word to the TCS. A data word, which contains the data, follows the status word.
The RTU decodes the address in the command word . When the RTU receives the data word from
the TCS, it sends the data to the location address and a status word to the TCS. This shows that
the write cycle is completed.

4.4 Examining the blanking pulse waveform
To help you identify faults you can examine the blanking pulse waveform.
The BLANKING signal is an output from the programmable pulse generator PCB in the HTCA. The
signal then goes through the serial link to the serial link isolation PCB. The BLANKING signal stops
serial communications between the TCS and the linear accelerator
• Immediately before the thyratron fires
• when the thyratron fires
• Immediately after the thyratron fires

2 Make sure that the serial link isolation PCB is insulated from the metal frame of the TCS
cabinet.
3 Use a probe to connect the oscilloscope between TP12 and TP9 on the serial link isolation
PCB .
4 Examine the waveforms and make sure that they are the equivalent of those in Figure 4.6
and Figure 4.7.

4.5 Correcting a PCB fault
The circuit diagrams of some PCBs are in the Linear Accelerator System Diagrams Manual.
To identify a fault with a PCB:
• you can interchange the PCB that you think is unserviceable with a serviceable PCB
from a different RTU or control area
• If the symptom transfers from one RTU or control area to the other, then the PCB you
think is unserviceable, has a fault.
The DIE PCBs and the SCC PCBs are not interchangeable items. If you think that you have an
unserviceable DIE or SCC PCB, it is necessary to install a new PCB to isolate the fault.
WARNING 4.1
Do not remove and install a PCB with the electrical supply on. If you remove or install a PCB
with the electrical supply on, you can get an electric shock. If you ignore this warning, you can
cause fatal injury.
CAUTION 4.1
Always use ESD handling procedures when you remove or install PCBs. If you ignore this
caution, you can cause damage to the equipment.

WARNING 5.1
Do not do work on the equipment after you disconnect the electrical supply before you do a

Service parts repair and replacement
Testing replacement system communications PCBs
5.7 Testing replacement system communications PCBs
There are no special tests after the repair of a system communications PCB.
1 Start the linear accelerator.
2 Operate the linear accelerator in the condition at which the system communications fault
occurred.
3 Make sure that the fault is corrected.

5.8 Description of the serial link cables in the reeling interface
cabinet
See the Linear Accelerator System Diagrams Manual, drawing 4513 330 7018, for more
information about the general layout of the interconnecting cables of the linear accelerator
All the serial link cables have the same electrical specification. Therefore if one of the serial
cables is defective, you can interchange most of the cable from the other RTU.
But, the cables between the TCC and the linear accelerator, and between the RTUs of the control
areas, are of different lengths. Therefore, Elekta recommends that you do not interchange these
cables.
The cable routing between the TCC (AREA 92), the interface cabinet (Area 70), and the cable
reeling post (AREA 60) is different in each installation. See the installation drawings for more
information.
